item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It took me a while to realize that there was no actual connection between the 2016 election and any of the last three titles, so I guess that's something. But you definitely got my upvote when I realized what Star Command Proton represented.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ronald Reagan as a comic book hero in space? That alone got my positive rating.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the dumbest fucking thing and it's terrifying.

item.title: Well...this has definitely aged well
item.content:encoded: The God-Emperor Threatens Annihilation!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 'Spam' or no, that rules.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You do realize that you replied to a comment that's 2 years old, right? From what I've heard, that counts as spam.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does the title "Donnie Darkmatter Strikes Sharat Spaceport!" sound like a good pun?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also, fuck me but it's election year. I'm going to have to rewrite me some POTUS Puns. I'll hold out on updating until Super Tuesday; enough people feel this SCP is shit without being out of date.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea was that this is a genuine comic book from the 1930s from an unknown manufacturer. It accurately (if loosely) depicts real-world US political events from the 1980s until current day. The Foundation is unable to determine how or why - is it time travel, some sort of psychic vision, or someone manipulating the events themselves? But the listed comics go beyond current events and extend into a possible timeline where Very Bad Things may happen. Star Command Proton is losing; Death To Mankind. It piques their interest to say the least.

Also I'm supremely disappointed that several years later, no one has noticed the Devestator Ray joke :( The .22LR ammunition Hinckley chose for the assassination was a more expensive type, advertised to fragment reliably and cause severe wound channels. The brand name? Devestator.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The description does mention that forensic analysis finds it to be genuine so it really was printed in 1932 predating the events it describes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I question why would this be an SCP. It is not directly anomalous, and there's nothing out of the ordinary other than it being pulp fiction versions of people in the future. For all we know, they could be made contemporarily in the style of black-and-white comics. It's like treating [Insert fictional franchise] is real just because they use RL names and events.

Yeah… downvoted…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The presidential references are definitely tongue in cheek and meant to be silly from a narrative point of view. The other side is that, in the world of the SCP itself, they are designed to be recognizable to an American living around the beginning of the 21st century. Presumably something mighty bad is coming, and the copies (if they exist) may contain valuable intel on the nature of some upcoming calamity. The use of sequential US Presidents also serves as a countdown. Each title means at most an eight year period (presumably), meaning every election is one term closer to A Very Bad Thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When writing this, I didn't put much thought into the Foundation only being a USA-centric body. It was more a sense of perspective - if (through time travel or prescience) someone wanted to provide cryptic clues to the Foundation via a pulp comic book in the 1930's, they would need to select a major attention-grabber and run with it. Obvious references to future US Presidents would be an obvious attention-grabber and flag the Foundation's attention in order to warn them of…. Well, I don't have a clue, and neither do they.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a matter of headcanon, really. Most people seem to go with the Foundation being based mostly in America with varying international presence, independent from the host governments, but acting with their approval. Considering that this site grew from a largely American userbase into a worldwide effort with fandoms of various sizes, the in-universe explanation isn't too far from the out-of-universe explanation.

Why did you post this on the discussion page for a random SCP, though? We have the foundation universe forum for a reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have a question about the Foundation. The intro says that it is a worldwide organization. Does this mean that it is an American organization that operates worldwide, as many articles seem to assume, or that it does not answer to any single government? The fact that it is a foundation would seem to make it the latter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well obviously Star Command Proton was named in memoriam of poor Jack, who died in the line of battle.
You know what would be cool? If someone wrote a tale with a throwaway reference to one of the last two, that could be fun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is certainly charming, but I feel like I've seen this before. Of someone else were to upvote this, though…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
New headcanon: SCP-1981-1 is a villian from this comic book who somehow escaped into the real world.

Also, there needs to be a Ronnie Ray-Gun / Jack Proton crossover.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, before the most recent reboot, Batman, Superman, and the rest were on New Earth. In the one before that one, however, you are pretty much spot on, and it is a very plausible idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm totally fine with someone wanting to do a picture for this article. I'm terrible with Photoshop myself.

Edit: Oh Jesus, that's awesome. Spaceman Hinckley shall always be burned into my mind as Dr. Manhattan now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You've added pictures to a lot of articles without permission today. Some were bad, some were good, but that's not the point. Just do the mods a favor and either find the original author and ask for permission or, if he/she are unreachable, ask the Senior Staff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I believe Wildt, the original author, is still around. You'll need to ask him for permission before adding it.

Honestly, though, I wouldn't recommend adding it either way; the idea's clever, but the image is rather eye-burning and difficult to read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of my faves. The "Starman Willy" bit is the kind of brilliant that makes you want to immediately run and share this with people.

I altered some comic featuring Reagan into this excerpt for a possible page image. I'll wait for feedback before actually adding it to the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's kind of a silly idea, but I like it. As DrArmstrong said farther up the page, it seems like something a time traveller would do as a joke, and I enjoyed the semi-easter egg: Star Command Proton having the initials SCP. Also, the advertisements at the end help make it less of a time traveller joke and more of a creepy time traveller warning. It might make a better -J, but I don't know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This comment is responsible for my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted just for the Franken vs Rush bit. Not a clue why that amused me as much as it did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And just like that upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In the DC comics universe, before the most recent reboot, we live on Earth-Prime, and Superman, Batman and the rest are on Earth-1.

What if, in the world of SCP-095, we are Earth-1? These comics were written for children for that universe, so, of course the facts might be skewed, just like many of our movies are rarely historically accurate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Would you still loathe it if it was an Anomalous Item?

I get the weird feeling that Moose is gonna bite your head off. Continue.

Your weird feeling is accurate.

Why would this being an Anomalous Item make any difference? "Anomalous" doesn't equal "boring", "anomalous" equals "does not merit further attention" for any number of reasons (I usually think of 'attention' as code for 'research'. If the idea is stupid as an SCP, the idea would also be stupid as an anomalous item.

All the items on the anomalous item list (that Light has allowed to stay there, that is - I can't speak for the crop of additions that she hasn't looked at yet) have some sort of charm or interesting element to them.

Note that this is a writing conceit, not an in-universe one. I'm sure the Foundation has catalogued reams of totally boring shit, but we don't need to hear about it unless the presentation is going to be interesting.

If I misunderstood your post, then feel free to ignore all this. Pet peeve. ;)

As for this article, I cannot help but like it, even though it's admittedly sorta dumb. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe not 9/11… but terrorists… the "Walker" part…

EDIT: I was making a Bush reference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You mean like, someone was attacked… Sneakily?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have now canceled my vote. While I do not like it enough to upvote it, I can find no logical reason to downvote it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like one of those comics was a 9/11 reference…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I get the weird feeling that Moose is gonna bite your head off. Continue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Would you still loathe it if it was an Anomalous Item?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I still loathe this stupid article, just sayin'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't like this, but noting that is a tiny mark in its favor.

But this is still assbad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
FFFFF-

I did not notice that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also, what are the initials of Star Command Proton? In that context, being in a losing battle is much more worrisome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My mistake.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now, if they had done all sorts of tests dating the comics to 1932, then it might be different, assuming that the tests were foolproof.

…

Forensic inspections of SCP-095 have revealed it to be genuine, though completely unremarkable save for its content.

By 'genuine', it means "what it claims to be". And it claims to be made in 1932.

assuming that the tests were foolproof

One test on its own could be flawed, yes. But doesn't this seem like the kind of thing you would test over and over, just to be sure? One test might fail, but ten tests are highly unlikely to fail.

Nor do I understand why it's important to recover the last ones. Is the Foundation trying to prevent events before they happen?

Wouldn't you? Or, if they can't prevent events, at least they can prepare.

If so, then why is 'Star Command Proton in a Losing Battle' worrisome, since it could just as easily refer to, say, Vietnam.

Assuming the future issues are listed in chronological order (which is a reasonable assumption), it could be referring to Iraq or Afghanistan. Of course, it might also refer to a legitimate military threat to the United States. I'm guessing that it's highly probable, especially considering that the next issue is

The New Menace – Death to Mankind!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I do not like this.

Yes, I know the publication date says 1932, but there's nothing to suggest that it's temporally displaced. You know what my first reaction would be upon seeing this? Someone's making a joke about the Star Wars (the Reagan version). Funny.

Seriously, there's no reason that this couldn't have been made by some person with nothing better to do.

Now, if they had done all sorts of tests dating the comics to 1932, then it might be different, assuming that the tests were foolproof.

Nor do I understand why it's important to recover the last ones. Is the Foundation trying to prevent events before they happen? If so, then why is 'Star Command Proton in a Losing Battle' worrisome, since it could just as easily refer to, say, Vietnam.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually like this one but would prefer if you made the presidential references less explicit and let the reader figure it out on their own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like that take on it a lot, actually.

That said, I hate this SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno, I like it. It seems like the kind of thing a time traveler would make as a joke.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree completely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Awesome, thanks for the feedback (and sorry for upvoting, whoops). Hopefully I can flesh out some other ideas in a week or two, ones that don't involve laser-slinging ex-Presidents.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't like this, and I'm trying hard to figure out why everyone else does… (?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is hilarious, and would make an excellent -J. The fact that it manages to work as a regular entry is even more impressive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …I really expected to dislike this one, I really did. This is like all the stupid "textbook from the future" ones, but it works. Partially because the "future" reference is so tiny, and partially because that's not the main point of it. Whatever it is, I like it a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one, but downvoted due to you upvoting your own article. Upvoting your own article is only to be done in cases of malacious downvoting.
